keyIdeas: The mole is the SI unit of amount of substance; 1 mole of any substance contains exactly 6.02 × 10²³ particles (the Avogadro constant, Nₐ).
Molar mass (M) is the mass of one mole of a substance in g mol⁻¹; numerically equal to the relative atomic or molecular mass.
The molar gas volume at RTP (room temperature and pressure, 25 °C and 100 kPa) is 24,000 cm³ mol⁻¹ (24 dm³ mol⁻¹).
Amount of substance n (in mol) links mass, molar mass, and the Avogadro constant: n = m / M.
For gases at RTP: n = V(cm³) / 24,000 or n = V(dm³) / 24.
Avogadro's constant allows conversion between moles and numbers of particles: N = n × Nₐ.
The terms 'amount of substance', 'mole', and 'molar mass' have precise A-level meanings — use them exactly as defined.

mustKnow.definition: A quantity measured in moles; n = m / M. It counts how many formula units of a substance are present.
The amount of substance that contains as many elementary entities as there are atoms in exactly 12 g of carbon-12; equal to 6.02 × 10²³ entities.
6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹; the number of particles per mole of substance.
The mass per mole of a substance, in g mol⁻¹; numerically equal to the relative atomic or molecular mass.
The volume occupied by one mole of any gas at a specified temperature and pressure; 24,000 cm³ mol⁻¹ (24 dm³ mol⁻¹) at RTP (25 °C, 100 kPa).

markSchemePhrases: amount of substance / number of moles
24,000 cm³ mol⁻¹ OR 24 dm³ mol⁻¹ at RTP
n = V / 24,000 (V in cm³) OR n = V / 24 (V in dm³)
commandWords: 'State' asks you to recall a definition or value with no working needed (e.g. 'State the value of the Avogadro constant'). 'Calculate' requires a numerical answer with units and full working shown. 'Define' requires a precise A-level statement — include units where applicable. 'Explain' asks you to link a concept to a reason using chemical principles.
examinerTips: Always quote Nₐ as 6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹ — do not omit the unit mol⁻¹.
Molar gas volume is 24,000 cm³ mol⁻¹ at RTP; check units in the question — volume may be given in dm³ or cm³.
Distinguish 'amount of substance' (moles, quantitative) from 'number of particles' (dimensionless integer ×  Nₐ).
If a 'define the mole' question appears, the definition must reference 6.02 × 10²³ particles (or 'as many entities as in 12 g of ¹²C') to score the mark.
RTP in OCR A means 25 °C and 100 kPa — do not use 24,400 cm³ mol⁻¹ (that is STP at 0 °C, 101.3 kPa).
workedExample:
Calculate the amount, in mol, of nitrogen gas (N₂) in 168 cm³ at RTP.

n = V / 24,000 = 168 / 24,000 = 7.00 × 10⁻³ mol

(3 significant figures to match the data; unit mol must be stated.)
commonTraps: Writing 'molecules' when the substance is ionic (use 'formula units' or 'ions'); 'mole of NaCl' contains 6.02 × 10²³ formula units, not molecules.
Confusing molar mass (g mol⁻¹, per mole) with relative molecular mass (dimensionless ratio) — the numerical values are equal but the units differ.
Using 22,400 cm³ mol⁻¹ (STP) instead of 24,000 cm³ mol⁻¹ (RTP) in OCR A questions.
Omitting mol⁻¹ from the Avogadro constant — Nₐ = 6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, not 6.02 × 10²³.
